title: Habitat Fragmentation Simulator
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Convert habitat cells to developed land and observe how fragmentation reduces populations, creates edge effects, and isolates wildlife.

learningObjectives: Demonstrate how fragmentation reduces effective habitat area and isolates populations
Explain the concept of edge effects and how they reduce interior habitat quality
Evaluate the effectiveness of wildlife corridors in maintaining population connectivity
Compare different development patterns and their relative impact on biodiversity
prerequisites: Basic knowledge of population ecology and carrying capacity
Familiarity with the concept of biodiversity

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Agent-based simulation with hands-on land-use decision making
keyQuestions: Is it better to preserve one large habitat or several small ones of the same total area?
How do edge effects reduce the effective size of a habitat patch?
Under what conditions can wildlife corridors restore population connectivity?
Which real-world development patterns cause the most fragmentation?
Why do small isolated populations face higher extinction risk?
commonMisconceptions: Students often assume that if 70% of habitat remains, 70% of species will survive (ignoring fragmentation effects)
Many students believe wildlife corridors always solve fragmentation problems regardless of width or habitat quality
Students may think edge effects only matter at the very border rather than penetrating several cells deep
assessmentOpportunities: Compare population outcomes across different preset scenarios with student-written explanations
Evaluate student policy recommendations for realistic development scenarios
Assess understanding of edge effects by having students calculate interior vs. total habitat area
